name: Simple Interest — Rent on Money
whatIsIt: Interest is the rent you pay to borrow money (or earn to lend it). SIMPLE interest is charged only on the original amount (the PRINCIPAL) every year — it never earns interest on interest. The formula is SI = P × R × T ÷ 100, where P is the principal, R the rate per year (in %), and T the time in years. The total you repay (the AMOUNT) is A = P + SI.
whyNeeded: Loans, savings, EMIs, gold loans and fixed deposits all start from interest. Simple interest is a guaranteed SSC, Bank and Railway aptitude topic and the base for understanding the more powerful compound interest.
realLifeUses: Borrowing ₹10,000 from a bank at 8% for 2 years
Interest earned on a savings deposit
A shopkeeper's short-term loan for stock
Comparing two loan offers before choosing one
SSC / Bank / Railway quantitative aptitude questions

vocabulary.meaning: The original sum borrowed or invested.
The percent charged per year, e.g. 8% per annum.
The number of years the money is borrowed/invested.
Principal plus interest: A = P + SI.

story.title: The Fair Loan
story.text: Rekha borrows ₹5,000 at 10% simple interest for 3 years. 'How much do I repay?' The clerk works it out: 'Each year the interest is 10% of ₹5,000 = ₹500. Over 3 years that's 3 × ₹500 = ₹1,500. So SI = 5000 × 10 × 3 ÷ 100 = ₹1,500, and the total AMOUNT is 5000 + 1500 = ₹6,500.' Rekha likes that the interest is the same every year — it's only ever on the original ₹5,000.
story.extractedProblem: ₹5,000 at 10% simple interest for 3 years — find the interest and the total amount.
story.answerInStory: SI = ₹1,500; Amount = ₹6,500 (interest is a flat ₹500 each year).

visual.caption: Simple interest is the SAME every year because it is always taken on the original principal. Amount = principal + interest.

teachingGallery.title: SI = PRT/100, Amount = P + SI
teachingGallery.note: The yearly interest is a flat P×R/100. Total interest = that × T.

standardMethod.summary: Multiply principal, rate and time, divide by 100 for the interest; add to principal for the amount.
steps: Write down P (principal), R (rate % per year), T (time in years).
If T is in months, convert to years (months ÷ 12) first.

alternateMethods.name: Yearly-interest method
steps: Multiply by the number of years.

workedExamples.problem: Find the simple interest on ₹8,000 at 5% per annum for 4 years.
₹1,200 is lent at 10% per annum. Find the total amount after 2 years.

trickPractice.intro: Find P×R/100 for one year, multiply by T; add to P for the amount.

commonMistakes.mistake: Computing P × R × T but forgetting to divide by 100 (treating the rate as a whole number, not a percent).
commonMistakes.fix: R is a PERCENT — always divide by 100. 10% means 10/100 = 0.1, so ₹1000 at 10% is ₹100, not ₹10,000.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reporting the interest when the question asks for the total amount (or vice versa).
commonMistakes.fix: Interest is the extra money (SI). Amount is principal + interest. Read carefully: 'how much interest' vs 'how much to repay / total amount'.

challenge.q: SI on ₹1200 at 10% for 6 months (₹)? (6 months = 0.5 year)
The question asks 'total amount to repay'. You should give…

questions.q: In simple interest, each year's interest is…

teachBackPrompt: Teach a friend simple interest: SI = PRT/100 and Amount = P + SI. Use ₹5000 at 10% for 3 years, show the flat ₹500 each year, and explain why simple interest never grows on itself.

revisionCard.summary: SI = P × R × T ÷ 100 (R is % per year, T in years). Amount = P + SI. Each year's interest is the SAME because it's always on the original principal. Convert months to years (÷12). Don't forget the ÷100, and know whether the question wants interest or total amount.

gameMission.title: The Fair Loan Desk
gameMission.brief: Run the loan desk! Compute each customer's flat yearly interest, total the amount, and dodge the two traps — forgetting ÷100 and mixing up interest with amount.

realLifeProject: Find a real savings or loan rate (ask a grown-up or check a bank site). Take a pretend ₹1,000 and work out the simple interest for 1, 2 and 3 years — notice the interest is the same each year.

rememberIt.unpack: Flat interest each year on the original principal. Divide by 100. Months ÷ 12 for years.

formulas.name: Simple interest
formulas.whenToUse: Interest on the original sum only.

studentQuestions.q: Why is simple interest the same every year?
studentQuestions.a: Because it is always calculated on the original principal, never on the interest already earned — so nothing grows year to year.

studentQuestions.q: What if the time is in months?
studentQuestions.a: Convert to years by dividing by 12 (e.g. 6 months = 0.5 year) before using the formula.
